result:
# The Quiet Art of Noticing

There is a small pleasure in watching light move across a room.

It is not dramatic. Nobody applauds it. No one writes breaking news about the afternoon sun slowly withdrawing from the floorboards, leaving behind a pale rectangle that thins, tilts, and disappears. Yet if you sit still long enough, if you resist the urge to check a phone or refill a cup or invent a task, the light becomes an event. The room changes. Dust rises and glitters in the air like a private weather system. A chair, ordinary five minutes ago, suddenly possesses a shadow with personality. The wall, which seemed blank, becomes a screen on which the day quietly performs.

I think we underestimate the world’s willingness to offer itself to us. Not in grand revelations, necessarily, but in small textures: the rubbery squeak of wet shoes on tile, the smell of oranges when someone begins peeling one across the room, the way a dog’s ears react before the rest of its body understands a sound, the tiny pause before rain begins in earnest. Life is densely packed with these signals, but we often move through them as though they are static between the “important” parts.

This is understandable. Modern life trains us to value the measurable, the productive, the shareable. We are encouraged to convert experience into evidence: photographs, updates, achievements, summaries, milestones. Even leisure is often expected to justify itself. Did the vacation restore us? Did the book improve us? Did the walk count toward our daily step goal? Did the conversation expand our network? We have learned to ask not only “What happened?” but “What did it do for me?”

Noticing is different. It is not opposed to productivity, but it refuses to be governed by it. To notice something is to grant it a moment of reality beyond its usefulness. A sparrow on a railing does not need to become a metaphor, though it may become one if we are the sort of people who cannot help ourselves. A cup of tea does not need to symbolize comfort, ritual, or class identity. It can simply be warm, bitter, fragrant, held between two hands while the morning gathers itself.

There is a kind of humility in attention. When we truly notice, we admit that the world is not merely a backdrop to our concerns. It has its own presence. The tree outside the window is not waiting to be incorporated into our mood. The stranger on the bus is not an extra in the film of our day. Even our own lives become stranger and richer when we stop assuming we already know what they contain.

Children are often excellent noticers, though not always in ways convenient to adults. A child walking down a sidewalk may stop for a beetle, a bottle cap, a crack shaped like a river, a piece of gum fossilized into the pavement. Adults, having “better things to do,” urge the child onward. But the child is not wasting time. The child is practicing intimacy with existence. The world has not yet been sorted into categories of relevance and irrelevance. Everything still has the possibility of being interesting.

Of course, children also miss things adults notice. They may not detect exhaustion in a parent’s voice, or the tension around a dinner table, or the cost of groceries. Noticing is not innocence. It is a skill that deepens, complicates, and sometimes burdens us. To notice beauty is one thing; to notice suffering is another. The more attentive we become, the less easily we can pretend that our comfort is isolated from the lives around us.

This may be one reason we avoid attention. Distraction is not merely a bad habit; it is sometimes a refuge. The world, fully perceived, can be overwhelming. To notice the loneliness of someone we love may require us to act. To notice our own dissatisfaction may require change. To notice injustice may demand that we surrender some convenient ignorance. There are forms of attention that cost us something.

Still, the alternative is a life skimmed rather than lived. We can pass through years collecting impressions without ever entering them. We can know the names of many things and the feel of very few. We can become experts in commentary and remain amateurs in presence.

I do not mean that we should all abandon ambition and spend our lives staring at clouds, though there are worse fates. The point is not to reject speed, technology, planning, or the complex machinery of contemporary life. The point is to remember that beneath all our systems, we remain creatures with senses, bodies, seasons, appetites, memories, and an astonishing capacity to be moved by what is near at hand.

There is an old temptation to imagine that meaning lives elsewhere. Elsewhere may be a future achievement, a different city, a more impressive career, a more glamorous circle of friends, a more enlightened version of ourselves. We say, “When I get there, then life will begin.” But life is notoriously unwilling to wait for our readiness. It begins constantly, inconveniently, in grocery lines and traffic jams, in kitchens, in waiting rooms, in the five minutes before a meeting, in the slow booting-up of an old laptop, in the silence after someone says something honest.

The ordinary is not the opposite of the meaningful. It is the place where meaning either appears or fails to appear.

Consider a morning routine. To one person, it is dull repetition: alarm, shower, coffee, keys, commute. To another, or to the same person on a different day, it is a chain of sensations and choices. The alarm is harsh, yes, but also proof of trust in tomorrow: last night, someone believed morning would come and made a plan for it. The shower is not merely hygiene but a small weather event under human control. Coffee is chemistry, agriculture, habit, comfort, addiction, culture, and smell. Keys are permission, responsibility, access. The commute is a daily migration through the lives of others.

Nothing has changed except attention, and yet everything has changed.

The danger here is that this begins to sound like advice, and advice can make even wonder feel like homework. “Be present” has become one of those phrases printed on mugs and wellness calendars until it risks meaning almost nothing. The command to be mindful can become another burden, another way to fail. You were distracted? You did not savor your lunch? You rushed through the sunset? Shame on you; you have mismanaged your consciousness.

That is not what I mean. Attention should not become a moral performance. Nobody can notice everything. Nobody should try. The mind must wander; boredom has its uses; routine protects us from sensory overload. Sometimes we need distraction because we are tired, grieving, overstimulated, or simply human. The art of noticing is gentle or it is nothing. It begins not with self-criticism but with invitation.

One way to practice is to choose a single thing and let it be enough. Not the whole world. Not the entire present moment in all its shimmering complexity. Just one thing.

Notice the soundscape of your room. Perhaps there is a refrigerator humming, a car passing outside, a pipe clicking, a computer fan breathing, someone laughing faintly through a wall. Notice how many sounds you had filtered out until now. Notice that silence, when examined, is rarely empty.

Or notice your hands. They are among the most loyal companions you have. They have carried bags, opened doors, typed messages, washed dishes, held other hands, touched foreheads, waved goodbye, steadied you against walls, learned the shapes of tools, books, faces, fabrics. Even if you dislike their appearance, even if they ache or tremble or bear scars, they are historical documents. They remember your life in calluses, lines, reflexes.

Or notice a familiar street. Walk it as though you had to describe it to someone who will never see it. How many trees are there? Which windows have plants? Where does the pavement buckle? What does the air smell like near the bakery, the laundromat, the bus stop after rain? Which doorways collect leaves? What colors are the signs? How many details have been waiting, patiently unobserved, in a place you thought you knew?

Attention reveals that familiarity is often a form of sleep.

This is true of people as well. We think we know those closest to us, and in many ways we do. We know their preferences, stories, wounds, jokes, allergies, passwords, the way they take coffee, the topics that make them defensive. But people are never finished. They are not solved by intimacy. A spouse of thirty years can still make an unfamiliar expression while reading a letter. A parent can reveal a fear you never suspected. A friend can change quietly, over months, while you continue addressing the older version of them.

To love someone well may be, in part, to keep noticing them.

This is harder than sentimental language suggests. The people nearest us are often the easiest to blur. We replace them with our expectations. We hear the first half of a sentence and assume the second. We respond to old arguments instead of present words. We stop asking real questions because we believe we already know the answers. Under the pressure of habit, even beloved faces become furniture.

But occasionally something interrupts us. A laugh catches us off guard. Illness makes the body newly visible. Distance sharpens affection. A photograph from years ago reveals how young everyone was, how much was happening beneath the surface, how little we understood at the time. Suddenly the familiar person becomes vivid again, and we feel a mixture of gratitude and regret: gratitude that they are here, regret that we had allowed them to become ordinary in the lesser sense of the word.

Perhaps this is why loss is such a brutal teacher of attention. When something is gone, its details often return with unbearable clarity. The sound of a key in the door. The particular rhythm of footsteps. The chipped mug no one else used. The phrase repeated so often it once annoyed us. Grief is, among other things, attention with nowhere to go. It gathers around absences and makes them bright.

This suggests that noticing is not merely aesthetic; it is ethical and existential. To notice is to honor the fact that things pass. The light moves across the room and vanishes. The child grows. The neighborhood changes. The body ages. The conversation ends. The season turns. Every ordinary moment is temporary, which does not make it tragic, exactly, but does make it precious in a way that cannot be postponed.

There is a Japanese phrase, mono no aware, often translated imperfectly as an awareness of impermanence or the pathos of things. It describes the tender sadness that arises from recognizing that beauty is fleeting. Cherry blossoms are beautiful not despite their brevity but partly because of it. Their falling is not a failure of the blossoms; it is inseparable from their meaning.

Western culture, particularly in its commercial forms, often tries to defeat impermanence through preservation, accumulation, and control. We archive everything. We photograph meals before tasting them, record concerts we barely watch directly, store thousands of images we may never revisit. There is nothing inherently wrong with this. Memory has always used tools. But sometimes documentation becomes a substitute for encounter. We become curators of experiences we have not fully had.

A photograph can deepen attention, of course. A painter, a photographer, a naturalist, a poet—such people often see more because their craft requires looking. The camera is not the enemy. The question is whether the tool serves attention or replaces it. Are we using the photograph to see, or are we using it to avoid the vulnerability of seeing?

To see directly is vulnerable because it cannot be possessed. The sunset you watch without photographing belongs to no archive. It leaves no proof. It exists in the fragile medium of your perception and memory. But perhaps that is not a weakness. Perhaps some experiences are meant to be received rather than captured.

The same is true of conversation. Some of the best conversations leave no transcript. They unfold late at night, or during a walk, or while washing dishes, and their power lies partly in their unrepeatability. Someone says something they did not know they believed until the words arrived. Someone else listens without preparing a rebuttal. A silence opens and is not immediately filled. The conversation changes the air between people, and afterward no one can quite summarize why it mattered.

Listening is one of the highest forms of noticing. It requires more than hearing words. It asks us to perceive tone, hesitation, emphasis, omission. It asks us to resist the gravitational pull of ourselves. While another person speaks, our own thoughts leap up eagerly: similar stories, counterarguments, advice, judgments, memories. Listening means letting those thoughts rise and pass without allowing them to seize the room.

To be listened to well is startling. It can feel almost medicinal. Many people carry within them a backlog of unreceived experience. They have spoken often but not necessarily been heard. When real listening occurs, something in the body recognizes it. Shoulders lower. Speech slows. The person becomes less defended, sometimes more precise. They may discover that what they needed was not a solution but a witness.

This, too, is part of the quiet art of noticing: becoming a better witness.

A witness does not consume experience like entertainment. A witness stands in relation to what is seen. This can apply to joy as much as pain. To witness someone’s delight without envy, to notice their enthusiasm and make room for it, is a generous act. Many joys wither because they are met with indifference or subtle competition. Someone shares good news, and immediately another person improves upon it, minimizes it, redirects it, or uses it as an occasion for self-comparison. But to say, in effect, “I see that this matters to you, and I am glad,” is to help joy become more real.

The world needs this sort of attention. Not only in private life but in public life as well. Much cruelty depends on abstraction. It is easier to dismiss people in categories than to notice them as particular human beings with names, histories, contradictions, and faces. Propaganda flattens. Prejudice blurs. Bureaucracy can blur too, even when staffed by decent people. The work of justice often begins by restoring detail: this person, this harm, this story, this consequence.

At the same time, attention without interpretation can be naive. Noticing must be joined with thought. To see smoke is important; to understand fire is also important. To notice someone’s suffering matters; to understand the conditions producing that suffering matters too. The art is to move between the particular and the structural without losing either. A hungry person is not merely an example of policy failure; they are a person who needs food. But if we only feed one person and never ask why so many are hungry, our attention remains incomplete.

So noticing is not an escape from the world’s complexity. It is an entrance into it.

Still, there is rest in it. When attention returns to the immediate, anxiety sometimes loosens its grip. Anxiety often lives in imagined futures, in rehearsals of disaster, in the mind’s attempt to control what cannot yet be touched. Noticing brings us back into contact with what is actual: the weight of the body in a chair, the pattern of breath, the blue edge of evening, the soup simmering, the pen in hand. The present may contain pain, but it usually contains more than pain. Attention widens the frame.

I once watched a storm approach across a flat landscape. At first there was only a darkening at the horizon, a smudge of blue-gray beneath a high white sky. Then the wind shifted. Leaves turned their pale undersides upward. Birds crossed the field with sudden purpose. The air acquired that metallic smell that seems less like a scent than a warning. Far off, rain descended in a visible column, moving toward us like a curtain being drawn across the earth.

For several minutes, everyone nearby stopped what they were doing. A delivery driver paused with one hand on the open door of his truck. A woman in a red jacket stood under a tree, looking outward. Two children pointed. The storm had not yet arrived, but its arrival was already happening. We were all, briefly, citizens of the same attention.

Then the rain came hard, and people ran.

I remember that more clearly than many supposedly important days. Nothing happened, in the usual sense. No decision was made, no relationship began or ended, no award was won, no crisis resolved. Weather moved over land. People looked up. Yet it remains in me as an image of shared human smallness, which is not the same as insignificance. To feel small before weather, oceans, mountains, stars, or time can be strangely relieving. It returns us to proportion.

Our lives are important, but they are not the only important thing. This is easy to say and hard to feel. We are each trapped, necessarily, inside a first-person perspective. Our toothaches hurt more than distant earthquakes. Our embarrassments loom larger than historical processes. Our plans feel central because they are central to us. Attention can both honor and soften this self-centeredness. It allows us to inhabit our own lives while also perceiving that the world exceeds them.

Nature is especially good at teaching this, though not because it is always gentle. Nature is not a spa brochure. It includes rot, predation, parasites, drought, flood, and indifference. But it also operates without consulting our opinions. The moon does not rise to inspire us. Moss does not grow for our aesthetic benefit. Bees do not pollinate as a lesson in cooperation. Their otherness is part of their gift. They free us, momentarily, from the exhausting belief that everything must be about us.

Yet even the human-made world contains more mystery than we grant it. A city sidewalk is a collaboration among engineers, laborers, weather, budgets, tree roots, spilled coffee, gum, footsteps, regulations, and time. A pencil contains cedar, graphite, mining, transport, design, childhood classrooms, standardized tests, sketches, carpenters’ marks, crossword puzzles, and the strange intimacy of an object shaped to fit the hand. A single loaf of bread implies soil, seed, rain, machinery, yeast, heat, appetite, and thousands of years of human experimentation.

To notice is to pull on a thread and discover that everything is woven.

This can become almost dizzying. The world is not a collection of isolated objects but a network of relations. The apple on the table contains an orchard, pollination, labor, sunlight, commerce, hunger, and the personal history of every apple you have ever eaten. The language in which you name it is older than you are and carries traces of vanished mouths. Even solitude is crowded with inheritance.

Maybe that is why gratitude so often follows attention. Not forced gratitude, not the brittle insistence that we must be thankful for everything, but a quieter recognition of dependence. We live by receiving. Air, water, food, language, care, knowledge, infrastructure, forgiveness: none of us invented the conditions of our own survival. Even the most independent person is held up by countless visible and invisible supports.

Gratitude, like attention, can be distorted into performance. But at its best it is simply accurate perception. It says: I did not make all this. I am not self-sufficient. Something has been given.

And if something has been given, perhaps something is asked.

What is asked may be as simple as care. Care for objects, places, people, words, tools, animals, one’s own body, one’s own commitments. Care is attention made durable. It is what happens when noticing continues over time. A neglected plant and a neglected friendship both reveal the cost of inattention. So does a democracy. So does a climate. So does a soul, if we may still use that word without embarrassment.

The soul—whatever else it may be—is surely affected by what we attend to. Spend hours each day immersed in outrage, comparison, and spectacle, and something in the inner life takes on those shapes. Spend time with music, honest work, difficult questions, trees, children, elders, silence, prayer, art, or service, and other shapes become possible. We become, gradually, what we give ourselves to.

This does not mean we can curate our way into purity. Life will bring ugliness, noise, triviality, and pain. Nor should we look only at beautiful things. But we have more agency than we sometimes admit. Attention is one of the basic materials from which a life is made. To spend it carelessly is to spend life carelessly.

The phrase “pay attention” is revealing. We pay attention as though attention were currency. It is limited. It can be invested, wasted, stolen, offered, demanded. Entire industries now compete to capture and monetize it. Our attention is valuable not only spiritually or philosophically, but economically. This makes the quiet act of choosing where to place it more significant than it may appear.

To look out a window instead of refreshing a feed can be a small rebellion. To read a difficult book slowly can be a rebellion. To have a conversation without glancing at a screen can be a rebellion. To notice the actual person in front of us rather than the category they represent can be a rebellion. To remain with grief, joy, boredom, or uncertainty without immediately numbing it can be a rebellion.

But rebellion is not the only language for it. It is also a homecoming.

We return to the senses. We return to the breath. We return to the strange fact of being alive at all. Against all improbability, we are here for a while, moving through days that are sometimes radiant, sometimes tedious, often both. We are given mornings and losses, errands and celebrations, bodies that fail and heal and fail again, people to misunderstand and be misunderstood by, chances to repair, chances to begin.

And always, somewhere, light is moving across a room.

Perhaps that is enough of a philosophy for one day: do not let the light move unnoticed every time. Not because you must become wise, not because every moment must be harvested for meaning, not because attention will save you from sorrow or make you morally superior. Simply because the light is there, and you are there, and the meeting of those two facts is your life.

Tomorrow there will be other demands. There will be messages to answer, bills to pay, mistakes to correct, meals to prepare, headlines to absorb, plans to revise. The world will rush in with its usual urgency. Some of that urgency will be real. Much of it will be manufactured. In the midst of it, perhaps there will be one small thing you can notice: steam rising from a cup, the tired kindness in someone’s face, the first yellow leaf of the season, the feeling of your feet on the ground.

Start there.

The world, which has been waiting all along, will offer another detail. Then another. Not everything at once. Just enough to remind you that you are not merely passing time.

You are living.

# The Quiet Art of Noticing

There is a small pleasure in watching light move across a room.

It is not dramatic. Nobody applauds it. No one writes breaking news about the afternoon sun slowly withdrawing from the floorboards, leaving behind a pale rectangle that thins, tilts, and disappears. This, too, is part of the quiet art of noticing: becoming a better witness.

A witness does not consume experience like entertainment. A witness stands in relation to what is seen. This can apply to joy as much as pain. To witness someone’s delight without envy, to notice their enthusiasm and make room for it, is a generous act. Many joys wither because they are met with indifference or subtle competition. Someone shares good news, and immediately another person improves upon it, minimizes it, redirects it, or uses it as an occasion for self-comparison. But to say, in effect, “I see that this matters to you, and I am glad,” is to help joy become more real.

The world needs this sort of attention. Not only in private life but in public life as well. Much cruelty depends on abstraction. It is easier to dismiss people in categories than to notice them as particular human beings with names, histories, contradictions, and faces. Propaganda flattens. Prejudice blurs. Bureaucracy can blur too, even when staffed by decent people. The work of justice often begins by restoring detail: this person, this harm, this story, this consequence.

At the same time, attention without interpretation can be naive. Noticing must be joined with thought. To see smoke is important; to understand fire is also important. To notice someone’s suffering matters; to understand the conditions producing that suffering matters too. The art is to move between the particular and the structural without losing either. A hungry person is not merely an example of policy failure; they are a person who needs food. But if we only feed one person and never ask why so many are hungry, our attention remains incomplete.

So noticing is not an escape from the world’s complexity. It is an entrance into it.

Still, there is rest in it. When attention returns to the immediate, anxiety sometimes loosens its grip. Anxiety often lives in imagined futures, in rehearsals of disaster, in the mind’s attempt to control what cannot yet be touched. Noticing brings us back into contact with what is actual: the weight of the body in a chair, the pattern of breath, the blue edge of evening, the soup simmering, the pen in hand. The present may contain pain, but it usually contains more than pain. Attention widens the frame.

I once watched a storm approach across a flat landscape. At first there was only a darkening at the horizon, a smudge of blue-gray beneath a high white sky. Then the wind shifted. Leaves turned their pale undersides upward. Birds crossed the field with sudden purpose. The air acquired that metallic smell that seems less like a scent than a warning. Far off, rain descended in a visible column, moving toward us like a curtain being drawn across the earth.

For several minutes, everyone nearby stopped what they were doing. A delivery driver paused with one hand on the open door of his truck. A woman in a red jacket stood under a tree, looking outward. Two children pointed. The storm had not yet arrived, but its arrival was already happening. We were all, briefly, citizens of the same attention.

Then the rain came hard, and people ran.

I remember that more clearly than many supposedly important days. Nothing happened, in the usual sense. No decision was made, no relationship began or ended, no award was won, no crisis resolved. Weather moved over land. People looked up. Yet it remains in me as an image of shared human smallness, which is not the same as insignificance. To feel small before weather, oceans, mountains, stars, or time can be strangely relieving. It returns us to proportion.

Our lives are important, but they are not the only important thing. This is easy to say and hard to feel. We are each trapped, necessarily, inside a first-person perspective. Our toothaches hurt more than distant earthquakes. Our embarrassments loom larger than historical processes. Our plans feel central because they are central to us. Attention can both honor and soften this self-centeredness. It allows us to inhabit our own lives while also perceiving that the world exceeds them.

Nature is especially good at teaching this, though not because it is always gentle. Nature is not a spa brochure. It includes rot, predation, parasites, drought, flood, and indifference. But it also operates without consulting our opinions. The moon does not rise to inspire us. Moss does not grow for our aesthetic benefit. Bees do not pollinate as a lesson in cooperation. Their otherness is part of their gift. They free us, momentarily, from the exhausting belief that everything must be about us.

Yet even the human-made world contains more mystery than we grant it. A city sidewalk is a collaboration among engineers, laborers, weather, budgets, tree roots, spilled coffee, gum, footsteps, regulations, and time. A pencil contains cedar, graphite, mining, transport, design, childhood classrooms, standardized tests, sketches, carpenters’ marks, crossword puzzles, and the strange intimacy of an object shaped to fit the hand. A single loaf of bread implies soil, seed, rain, machinery, yeast, heat, appetite, and thousands of years of human experimentation.

To notice is to pull on a thread and discover that everything is woven.

This can become almost dizzying. The world is not a collection of isolated objects but a network of relations. The apple on the table contains an orchard, pollination, labor, sunlight, commerce, hunger, and the personal history of every apple you have ever eaten. The language in which you name it is older than you are and carries traces of vanished mouths. Even solitude is crowded with inheritance.

Maybe that is why gratitude so often follows attention. Not forced gratitude, not the brittle insistence that we must be thankful for everything, but a quieter recognition of dependence. We live by receiving. Air, water, food, language, care, knowledge, infrastructure, forgiveness: none of us invented the conditions of our own survival. Even the most independent person is held up by countless visible and invisible supports.

Gratitude, like attention, can be distorted into performance. But at its best it is simply accurate perception. It says: I did not make all this. I am not self-sufficient. Something has been given.

And if something has been given, perhaps something is asked.

What is asked may be as simple as care. Care for objects, places, people, words, tools, animals, one’s own body, one’s own commitments. Care is attention made durable. It is what happens when noticing continues over time. A neglected plant and a neglected friendship both reveal the cost of inattention. So does a democracy. So does a climate. So does a soul, if we may still use that word without embarrassment.

The soul—whatever else it may be—is surely affected by what we attend to. Spend hours each day immersed in outrage, comparison, and spectacle, and something in the inner life takes on those shapes. Spend time with music, honest work, difficult questions, trees, children, elders, silence, prayer, art, or service, and other shapes become possible. We become, gradually, what we give ourselves to.

This does not mean we can curate our way into purity. Life will bring ugliness, noise, triviality, and pain. Nor should we look only at beautiful things. But we have more agency than we sometimes admit. Attention is one of the basic materials from which a life is made. To spend it carelessly is to spend life carelessly.

The phrase “pay attention” is revealing. We pay attention as though attention were currency. It is limited. It can be invested, wasted, stolen, offered, demanded. Entire industries now compete to capture and monetize it. Our attention is valuable not only spiritually or philosophically, but economically. This makes the quiet act of choosing where to place it more significant than it may appear.

To look out a window instead of refreshing a feed can be a small rebellion. To read a difficult book slowly can be a rebellion. To have a conversation without glancing at a screen can be a rebellion. To notice the actual person in front of us rather than the category they represent can be a rebellion. To remain with grief, joy, boredom, or uncertainty without immediately numbing it can be a rebellion.

But rebellion is not the only language for it. It is also a homecoming.

We return to the senses. We return to the breath. We return to the strange fact of being alive at all. Against all improbability, we are here for a while, moving through days that are sometimes radiant, sometimes tedious, often both. We are given mornings and losses, errands and celebrations, bodies that fail and heal and fail again, people to misunderstand and be misunderstood by, chances to repair, chances to begin.

And always, somewhere, light is moving across a room.

Perhaps that is enough of a philosophy for one day: do not let the light move unnoticed every time. Not because you must become wise, not because every moment must be harvested for meaning, not because attention will save you from sorrow or make you morally superior. Simply because the light is there, and you are there, and the meeting of those two facts is your life.

Tomorrow there will be other demands. There will be messages to answer, bills to pay, mistakes to correct, meals to prepare, headlines to absorb, plans to revise. The world will rush in with its usual urgency. Some of that urgency will be real. Much of it will be manufactured. In the midst of it, perhaps there will be one small thing you can notice: steam rising from a cup, the tired kindness in someone’s face, the first yellow leaf of the season, the feeling of your feet on the ground.

Start there.

The world, which has been waiting all along, will offer another detail. Then another. Not everything at once. Just enough to remind you that you are not merely passing time.

You are living.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
